     CS-404 Big Data Analytics
        Driver Code




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                  Association Rule Mining
Frequent Pattern. a pattern (a set of items, subsequences,
substructures, etc.) that occurs frequently in a dataset

Goal. Finding inherent regularities in data
    What products were often purchased together? Juice and diapers?
    What are the subsequent purchases after buying a PC?
    What kinds of DNA are sensitive to this new drug?
    Can we automatically classify Web documents

Applications
Basket data analysis, cross-marketing, catalog design, sale campaign
analysis, Web log (click stream) analysis, and DNA sequence analysis.



                          CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                         Frequent Pattern
Itemset. A set of one or more items
    K-itemset X = {x1 ,···,xk }

    (absolute) support, or, support count of X: Frequency or
    occurrence of an itemset X
    (relative) support, s, is the fraction of transactions that contains
    X (i.e., the probability that a transaction contains X)

An itemset X is frequent if X’s support is no less than a minsup
threshold.




                           CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                       Association Rules

Q. Find all the rules X → Y with minimum support and confidence
threshold



    Support, s, probability that a transaction contains X ∪Y
    Confidence, c, conditional probability that a transaction having
    X also contains Y




                         CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                  Association Rule Mining
Q. How many itemsets are potentially to be generated in the worst
case?
    The number of frequent itemsets to be generated is sensitive to
    the minsup threshold
    When minsup is low, there exist potentially an exponential number
    of frequent itemsets

The worst case: MN where M: # distinct items, and N : max length of
transactions




                          CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                   Association Rule Mining
The downward closure property of frequent patterns
    Any subset of a frequent itemset must be frequent
    If {Juice, Diaper, Nuts} is frequent, so is {Juice, Diaper}
    i.e., every transaction having {Juice, Diaper, Nuts} also contains
    {Juice, Diaper}

Apriori pruning principle
If there is any itemset which is infrequent, its superset should not be
generated/tested.




                           CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                        Apriori Method

1 Initially, scan DB once to get frequent 1-itemset
2 Generate length (k + 1) candidate itemsets from length k frequent
  itemsets
3 Test the candidates against DB
4 Terminate when no frequent or candidate set can be generated




                        CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                          Apriori Algorithm

C k : Candidate itemset of size k
Lk : frequent itemset of size k
L1 = frequent items;
for (k = 1;Lk ! = φ;k + +) do begin
      C k +1 = candidates generated from Lk ;
      for each transaction t in database do
             increment the count of all candidates in Ck +1 that are contained in t
      Lk +1 = candidates in Ck +1 with min_support
end
         S
return       k Lk ;




                             CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                          Candidate Generation

     How to generate candidates?
          S tep 1. self-joining Lk
          Step 2. pruning
Join Lk p with Lk q, as follows:
     insert into C k +1
     select {p.itemi }{1,···,k −1} ,p.itemk ,q.itemk
     from Lk p,Lk q
     where {p.itemi }{1,···,k−1} = {q.itemi }{1,···,k−1} and p.itemk < q.itemk




                               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
             Example: Apriori Algorithm

Database
                           Tid          Items
                           10           A, C, D
                           20           B, C, E
                           30          A, B, C, E
                           40             B, E

Min. Supp = 2
    1st scan

                                                        Itemset
   Itemset   sup
                           Itemset          sup
                                                         {A, B}
     {A}      2
                              {A}            2           {A, C}
     {B}      3    ⇒          {B}            3
                                                    ⇒    {A, E}
     {C}      3               {C}            3           {B, C}
     {D}      1
     {E}      3                  {E}          3          {B, E}
                                                         {C, E}
                       CS-404 Big Data Analytics
             Example: Apriori Algorithm

Database
                           Tid       Items
                           10      A, C, D
                           20      B, C, E
                           30     A, B, C, E
                           40        B, E

Min. Supp = 2
    2nd scan

   Itemset   sup
                           Itemset      sup
    {A, B}    1
    {A, C}    2             {A, C}       2             Itemset
    {A, E}    1    ⇒        {B, C}       2
                                                   ⇒
    {B, C}    2                                        {B, C, E}
                            {B, E}       3
    {B, E}    3             {C, E}       2
    {C, E}    2

                       CS-404 Big Data Analytics
           Example: Apriori Algorithm

Database

                       Tid         Items
                       10      A, C, D
                       20      B, C, E
                       30     A, B, C, E
                       40        B, E


Min. Supp = = 2
    3rd scan

                       Itemset       sup
                       {B, C, E}      2




                   CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                      Association Rules

Once the frequent itemsets have been found, it is straightforward
to generate strong association rules that satisfy:
    minimum support
    minimum confidence
Relation between Support and Confidence
                                              support_count(A ∪B )
          Confidence(A ⇒ B ) = P (B |A ) =
                                                support_count (A )


    Support_count(A ∪B ) is the number of transactions containing the
    itemsets A ∪B
    Support_count(A) is the number of transactions containing the
    itemset A.




                        CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example




Min. Sup. = 2




                CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example




Min. Sup. = 2




                CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                              Example




•   {I1, I2, I3}  {I1, I2}, {I1, I3}, and {I2, I3} are frequent, so keep it.
•   {I1, I2, I5}  Keep it as well
•   {I1, I3, I5}  {I3, I5}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I3, I4}  {I3, I4}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I3, I5}  {I3, I5}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I4, I5}  {I4, I5}? (remove it)

                              CS-404 Big Data Analytics
          Example




Dataset




          CS-404 Big Data Analytics
            Example: Patterns to Rules

   Suppose the frequent Itemset
   L = {I1 ,I2 ,I5 }                              Transactional Database
   Subsets of L are:
   {I1,I2}, {I1,I5}, {I2,I5}, {I1},{I2}, {I5}                   TID    List of item IDS
                                                                T100       I1 ,I2 ,I5
   Association rules:                                           T200         I2 ,I4
  I1 ∧I2 ⇒ I5 confidence = 2/ 4 = ?%                            T300         I2 ,I3
  I1 ∧I5 ⇒ I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T400       I1 ,I2 ,I4
  I2 ∧I5 ⇒ I1 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T500         I1 , I3
  I1 ⇒ I 2 ∧I 5 confidence = 2/ 6 = ?%                          T600         I2 , I3
                                                                T700         I1 ,I3
  I2 ⇒ I1 ∧I5 confidence = 2/ 7 = ?%                            T800     I1 ,I2 ,I3 ,I5
  I5 ⇒ I1 ∧I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T900       I1 ,I2 ,I3




Question: If the minimum confidence = 70%, what are the strong rules?
                                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
            Example: Patterns to Rules

   Suppose the frequent Itemset
   L = {I1 ,I2 ,I5 }                              Transactional Database
   Subsets of L are:
   {I1,I2}, {I1,I5}, {I2,I5}, {I1},{I2}, {I5}                   TID    List of item IDS

   Association rules:                                           T100       I1 ,I2 ,I5
                                                                T200         I2 ,I4
   I1 ∧I2 ⇒ I5 confidence = 2/ 4 = 50%                          T300         I2 ,I3
                                                                T400       I1 ,I2 ,I4
   I1 ∧I5 ⇒ I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%                                      I1 , I3
                                                                T500
   I2 ∧I5 ⇒ I1 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%                         T600         I2 , I3
                                                                T700         I1 ,I3
   I1 ⇒ I 2 ∧I 5 confidence = 2/ 6 = 33%
                                                                T800     I1 ,I2 ,I3 ,I5
   I2 ⇒ I1 ∧I5 confidence = 2/ 7 = 29%                          T900       I1 ,I2 ,I3
   I5 ⇒ I1 ∧I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%


Question: If the minimum confidence = 70%, what are the strong rules?
                                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics

Distributed Association Rule Mining


         Dr. Syed Imran Ali

    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                          Roadmap

• Recap!

• Distributed Apriori

• An improvement in Apriori memory
  consumption

• Yet another way of generating frequent
  patterns

                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example




Min. Sup. = 2




                CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                              Example




•   {I1, I2, I3}  {I1, I2}, {I1, I3}, and {I2, I3} are frequent, so keep it.
•   {I1, I2, I5}  Keep it as well
•   {I1, I3, I5}  {I3, I5}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I3, I4}  {I3, I4}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I3, I5}  {I3, I5}? (remove it)
•   {I2, I4, I5}  {I4, I5}? (remove it)

                              CS-404 Big Data Analytics
          Example




Dataset




          CS-404 Big Data Analytics
            Example: Patterns to Rules

   Suppose the frequent Itemset
   L = {I1 ,I2 ,I5 }                              Transactional Database
   Subsets of L are:
   {I1,I2}, {I1,I5}, {I2,I5}, {I1},{I2}, {I5}                   TID    List of item IDS
                                                                T100       I1 ,I2 ,I5
   Association rules:                                           T200         I2 ,I4
  I1 ∧I2 ⇒ I5 confidence = 2/ 4 = ?%                            T300         I2 ,I3
  I1 ∧I5 ⇒ I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T400       I1 ,I2 ,I4
  I2 ∧I5 ⇒ I1 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T500         I1 , I3
  I1 ⇒ I 2 ∧I 5 confidence = 2/ 6 = ?%                          T600         I2 , I3
                                                                T700         I1 ,I3
  I2 ⇒ I1 ∧I5 confidence = 2/ 7 = ?%                            T800     I1 ,I2 ,I3 ,I5
  I5 ⇒ I1 ∧I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = ?%                            T900       I1 ,I2 ,I3




Question: If the minimum confidence = 70%, what are the strong rules?
                                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
            Example: Patterns to Rules

   Suppose the frequent Itemset
   L = {I1 ,I2 ,I5 }                              Transactional Database
   Subsets of L are:
   {I1,I2}, {I1,I5}, {I2,I5}, {I1},{I2}, {I5}                   TID    List of item IDS

   Association rules:                                           T100       I1 ,I2 ,I5
                                                                T200         I2 ,I4
   I1 ∧I2 ⇒ I5 confidence = 2/ 4 = 50%                          T300         I2 ,I3
                                                                T400       I1 ,I2 ,I4
   I1 ∧I5 ⇒ I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%                                      I1 , I3
                                                                T500
   I2 ∧I5 ⇒ I1 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%                         T600         I2 , I3
                                                                T700         I1 ,I3
   I1 ⇒ I 2 ∧I 5 confidence = 2/ 6 = 33%
                                                                T800     I1 ,I2 ,I3 ,I5
   I2 ⇒ I1 ∧I5 confidence = 2/ 7 = 29%                          T900       I1 ,I2 ,I3
   I5 ⇒ I1 ∧I2 confidence = 2/ 2 = 100%


Question: If the minimum confidence = 70%, what are the strong rules?
                                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Improving Apriori (PCY)




    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
PCY-Algorithm

CS-404 Big Data Analytics
PCY-Algorithm




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                    Example
Support s = 3
Items: milk(1), coke(2), bread(3), pepsi(4), juice(5)

Transactions:
T1 = {1, 2, 3}
T2 = {1, 4, 5}
T3 = {1, 3},
T4 = {2, 5}
T5 = {1, 3, 4}
T6 = {1, 2, 3, 5}
T7 = {2, 3, 5}
T8 = {2, 3}
                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example

Pass 1
Step 1. Items count: counts how many
times each item appears (s = 3)

Result:
1:         5
2:         5
3:         6
4:         2
5:         4

(item 4 is below s}
               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example

Pass 1
Step 2. Make hash table for bucket counts

•   Create possible pairs of all items
•   Hash each pair in hash table
    (keep count only)
      • Hashing rule: hashing a pair {i,j} to a
         bucket k, where:
            • k = hash(i,j) = (i+j) % 5



               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
               Example

Pass 1

2. Make hash table for bucket counts

(1,4) and (2,3)  k=0
(1,5) and (2,4)  k=1
(2,5) and (3,4)  k=2
(1,2) and (3,5)  k=3
(1,3) and (4,5)  k=4



               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                    Example
Pass 1

2. Make hash table for bucket counts

T1 = {1, 2, 3}                                  |(1,2)3 (2,3)0 (1,3)4
T2 = {1, 4, 5}
T3 = {1, 3},
T4 = {2, 5}
T5 = {1, 3, 4}
T6 = {1, 2, 3, 5}
T7 = {2, 3, 5}
T8 = {2, 3}
                    CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                         Example
Pass 1

2. Make hash table for bucket counts

T1 = {1, 2, 3}                |(1,2)3 (2,3)0 (1,3)4
T2 = {1, 4, 5}                |(1,4)0 (1,5)1 (4,5)4
T3 = {1, 3},                  |(1,3)4
T4 = {2, 5}                   |(2,5)2
T5 = {1, 3, 4}                |(1,3)4 (3,4)2 (1,4)0
T6 = {1, 2, 3, 5}             |(1,2)3 (1,3)4 (1,5)1
                              |(2,3)0 (2,5)2 (3,5)3
T7 = {2, 3, 5}                |(2,3)0 (2,5)2 (3,5)3
T8 = {2, 3}                   |(2,3)0



                        CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Example

Pass 1 (after the completion)

•   Hash table results
     Bucket                  Count
     0                       6
     1                       2
     2                       4
     3                       4
     4                       5
               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                         Example
Pass 1

•       Hash table results

    Bucket       Count            •      The only pairs hashed to
                                         bucket 1 are (1,5) in t2
    0            6
                                         and (1,5) in t6  count 2
    1            2
    2            4                •      (1,5) in bucket 1 is not
                                         frequent
    3            4
    4            5
                         CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                      Example
Pass 2

•   Frequent items are {1, 2, 3, 5} (by pass 1 item’s
    count)

•   Possible candidate pairs are: (1,2) (1,3) (1,5) (2,3)
    (2,5) (3,5)

•   (1,5) is discarded because bucket 1 is not frequent

•   Surviving candidate pairs:
                   (1,2) (1,3)(2,3) (2,5) (3,5)

                     CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                      Example
Pass 2

•   Surviving pairs: (1,2) (1,3)(2,3) (2,5) (3,5)
•   Actual Count (via DB scan):
      • (1,2)            2
      • (1,3)            4
      • (2,3)            4
      • (2,5)            3
      • (3,5)            2

      •   -> (1,2) and (3,5) don’t exceed s

      •   Result: frequent items are:
             {1}, {2}, {3}, {5}, {1,3}, {2,3}, {2,5}
                      CS-404 Big Data Analytics
             Another Example




 Dataset




Minimum
Support: 2

                CS-404 Big Data Analytics
         Example
Tids   Item Pairs
T1     (1,2), (1,5), (2,5)
T2     (2,4)
T3     (2,3)
T4     (1,2), (1,4), (2,4)
T5     (1, 3)
T6     (2, 3)
T7     (1, 3)
T8     (1,2), (1,3), (1,5), (2,3), (2,5), (3,5)
T9     (1,2),   (1,5), (2,5)
        CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Hash: h(x,y) = ((order of x) * 10 + (order of y)) mod 7
 Buckets       Item Pairs                       Bucket count
 0             (1,4), (3,5)                                2
 1             (1,5), (1,5)                                2
 2             (2,3), (2,3), (2,3), (2,3)                  4
 3             (2,4), (2,4)                                2
 4             (2,5), (2,5)                                2
 5             (1,2), (1,2), (1,2), (1,2)                  4
 6             (1, 3), (1,3), (1,3), (1,3)                 4

 Buckets: all frequent!
 C2 = (1,4), (3,5), (1,5), (2,3), (2,4), (2,5), (1,2), (1,3)
 L2 = (1,5), (2,3), (2,4), (2,5), (1,2), (1,3)
                               CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                Frequent Pattern - Growth

• Adopts a divide and conquer strategy
• Compress the database representing frequent items into a
• frequent–pattern tree or FP-tree
    •   Retains the itemset association information

• Divide the compressed database into a set of conditional
  databases, each associated with one frequent item

• Mine each such databases separately




                            CS-404 Big Data Analytics
              FP-Growth Example
•   The first scan of data is the same as Apriori
•   Derive the set of frequent 1-itemsets
•   Let min-sup=2
•   Generate a set of ordered items




                        CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
Example




CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                  FP-Growth In Short

•   FP-growth transforms the problem of finding long
    frequent patterns to searching for shorter ones
    recursively and concatenating the suffix
•   It uses the least frequent suffix offering a good
    selectivity
•   It reduces the search cost
•   If the tree does not fit into main memory, partition the
    database
•   Efficient and scalable for mining both long and short
    frequent patterns


                      CS-404 Big Data Analytics
                    Distributed ARM
                                   TID             Items
                                   T1              ABC
•   Transactions: 12               T2              AB

•   Min. Supp: 4                   T3              AC
                                   T4              BC
•   Partitions: 3                  T5              ABC
                                   T6              ABD
                                   T7              BCD
Do It Yourself                     T8              ACD
                                   T9              ABCD
                                   T10             BC
                                   T11             AB
                                   T12             AC

                       CS-404 Big Data Analytics
